30 min read
Just now

--

Toward a synchronous quantum-hybrid machine — Part 2: path-indexed Rauf local execution
Don Gunter — WodansEye Research Series — Verified design note and Qiskit prototype — Revision 2, July 22, 2026
Companions: “A Bit-Level NAND Memory Bus — Part 1”; “The Rauf Quantum Local Machine.”

Purpose and result
Part 1 built a classical NAND memory and established the bus rule:
Address selects. Data transfers separately.
Part 2 crosses the first real quantum boundary.
The question is no longer whether a quantum NAND can produce one Boolean result. It is whether several NAND results can remain inside a coherent execution interval, feed one another, update recurrent state, select different local models, and preserve how many cycles each path executed before anything returns to the classical machine.
The tested answer is yes, within a bounded reversible circuit.
The prototype implements:
NAND A = NAND(x, current state)
NAND B = NAND(y, current state)
NAND C = NAND(A, B)
next state = CNAND C is not measured as the final answer. It becomes the state used by the next pass.
A path qubit selects between two minimal local models without measurement:
Path 0 → local model M0 → x=1, y=0 → the live state continues
Path 1 → local model M1 → x=0, y=0 → the state becomes zero and haltsThe circuit is unrolled for a fixed maximum number of cycle positions, but every path carries its own active history. One path may execute all sixteen positions while another stops after one. Both histories exist together in a single unitary branching state until the final measurement.
For a sixteen-cycle window, exact simulation produces one state containing both correlated outcomes:
M0 → count 16, final state 1, still active
M1 → count 1, final state 0, haltedEach outcome has probability 0.5 because the path register begins in an equal superposition. Path, model behavior, cycle count, and final state remain correlated until the commit boundary.
The implementation provides two counting methods:

• History-tape count — default. Each active[t] qubit is already a record that cycle t executed. The final classical commit counts those bits. No quantum addition occurs inside the loop.

• Binary count — reference. A reversible binary counter increments during every active cycle. It uses fewer count qubits for very long bounds, but adds carry logic.
The history tape is both loop control and quantum memory. It is the cleanest current answer to:
How many times did this path execute before returning to classical control?
This is execution, not merely search.
One boundary is stated up front, because it governs what Part 3 can do. The recorded history makes the two branches mutually distinguishable, and distinguishable branches cannot interfere. Section 9 measures this precisely. Part 2 delivers coherent execution, not interferable amplitudes.

Claim labels

• [BUILT] — implemented in rauf_coherent_nand_loop.py.

• [TESTED] — compared with a classical truth model and verified by exact and sampled simulation.

• [MEASURED] — reproduced by the included resource-audit command on the stated software versions.

• [DESIGN] — a proposed architectural interpretation or next implementation stage.

• [OPEN] — an unresolved research or engineering problem.
Reproduction note
Every quantitative result below was independently re-executed on a clean environment (Python 3.12, Qiskit 2.5.0, Qiskit Aer 0.17.2) during pre-publication review. Where a number is sensitive to toolchain configuration, that sensitivity is stated at the table rather than hidden. Readers must use the companion file version that includes the --counter and --audit options; earlier drafts of the program lacked both.

1. The correction that makes the loop possible
A classical NAND performs an irreversible mapping:
a, b → NAND(a, b)The output description discards the inputs. A coherent quantum circuit cannot generally discard information; its operations must be reversible.
The quantum NAND used here therefore preserves both inputs and XORs the NAND result into a target qubit:
|a, b, target⟩ → |a, b, target XOR NAND(a, b)⟩When the target starts at zero:
|a, b, 0⟩ → |a, b, NAND(a, b)⟩The controlled implementation is exact:
def enabled_nand(circuit, enable, left, right, target):
circuit.cx(enable, target)
circuit.mcx([enable, left, right], target)When enable=1, the target receives 1 XOR (left AND right), which is NAND. When enable=0, neither operation changes the target.
The operation is its own inverse: running the same gates again removes the temporary result and returns the target to zero. This gives the machine a reversible NAND that can participate in a longer coherent calculation without intermediate measurement.
[BUILT] [TESTED]

2. One local execution cycle
At cycle t the machine has a recurrent state bit state[t]. It computes:
A[t] = NAND(x, state[t])
B[t] = NAND(y, state[t])
C[t] = NAND(A[t], B[t])
state[t+1] = C[t]The complete Boolean transition simplifies to:
C[t] = state[t] AND (x OR y)The circuit deliberately does not substitute the simplified expression. It performs NAND A, NAND B, and NAND C so the three-NAND execution structure remains visible, testable, and replaceable.
The simplified form is useful for checking behavior. When the current state is zero, the next state remains zero. When the state is one and either local input is one, the state remains one. When the state is one and both local inputs are zero, the next state becomes zero. That supplies a minimal local continue-or-halt transition.

3. The first Rauf local-model bank
One path qubit acts as a one-bit local-model address:
path=0 → M0 path=1 → M1The two models are intentionally small. They are not yet adaptive filters. They demonstrate the architecture in which each path selects a different local state transition.
Model M0 — continue (x=1, y=0). For a live state: A = NAND(1,1) = 0, B = NAND(0,1) = 1, C = NAND(0,1) = 1. NAND C is one, so the recurrent state remains live.
Model M1 — halt (x=0, y=0). For a live state: A = NAND(0,1) = 1, B = NAND(0,1) = 1, C = NAND(1,1) = 0. NAND C is zero, so the path halts after its first active pass.
Selection without measurement. The path register begins in an equal superposition, and the circuit prepares x = NOT(path), y = 0. The two model behaviors therefore both occur within one unitary evolution: path 0 carries x=1 and executes M0, path 1 carries x=0 and executes M1. No measurement selects the model inside the loop; path, model input, recurrent state, and execution history become correlated.
This is the smallest executable form of the Rauf local-machine rule:
A path identifies a local model, and the local model determines the next local state.
[BUILT] [TESTED]

4. C is stored and looped back
The crucial step:
C output is not the answer.
C output is the next state.The circuit writes NAND C into a fresh state qubit and into the next active bit:
circuit.cx(scratch_c, state[t + 1])
circuit.cx(scratch_c, active[t + 1])Therefore C=1 means the next state is live and the next pass executes; C=0 means the state is zero and all later passes are inactive.
A fresh state and active qubit is allocated for each possible cycle. This history tape is the reversible equivalent of a bounded feedback loop: it avoids irreversible overwrite, so prior states remain available and the whole transformation can still be inverted.
The active history also records execution count. A path that executes k cycles leaves active[0:k] = 1 and active[k:R] = 0. In a sixteen-cycle window:
16 active cycles → 1111111111111111
5 active cycles → 1111100000000000
1 active cycle → 1000000000000000That is a unary counter already present in the loop memory.
[BUILT] [TESTED]

5. Compute, store, and uncompute
After NAND C has been written into the next-state and next-active qubits, the temporary NAND outputs are no longer needed. They are removed by applying the three reversible NAND operations in reverse order:
Compute NAND A, NAND B, NAND C.
Store C in state[t+1] and active[t+1].
Uncompute NAND C, NAND B, NAND A.The result is scratch A = scratch B = scratch C = 0, while state[t+1], active[t+1], and the path identity remain. In binary-counter mode the compact count also remains.
This is the standard reversible pattern: compute, copy the required result, uncompute temporary work. The test suite verifies that all three scratch qubits are zero at the final measurement for every valid path.
[BUILT] [TESTED]

6. Counting cycles without leaving the QPU
6.1 History-tape count — the default
The active tape already answers whether each possible cycle executed:
active[t] = 1 → cycle t executed
active[t] = 0 → cycle t did not executeNo ripple carry is required and no count register is updated. The circuit stores one execution bit per possible cycle and counts the ones only after final measurement. This is a direct quantum version of the Part 1 memory principle: the cycle position selects a memory location, and the active status is the stored data.
Advantages: no quantum addition during the loop; lower two-qubit depth; fewer qubits; a complete timing history rather than only a total; and natural halt-position inspection.
Cost: one active-history qubit per possible cycle, and a final measurement width that grows linearly with the cycle bound.
6.2 Binary count — retained reference
The program can instead increment a little-endian binary register on each active cycle:
def controlled_increment(circuit, enable, bits):
for index in range(len(bits) - 1, -1, -1):
controls = [enable, *bits[:index]]
if len(controls) == 1:
circuit.cx(controls[0], bits[index])
else:
circuit.mcx(controls, bits[index])For a maximum of sixteen passes, five counter qubits represent zero through sixteen. The binary counter is compact, but its multi-controlled carry operations synthesize into substantial two-qubit depth.
6.3 Exact equivalence
The tests run both counting modes at 1, 4, 8, and 16 rounds. They produce identical decoded outcomes and identical probabilities to ten decimal places:
M0 → active cycles = R
M1 → active cycles = 1The difference is resource use, not semantics.
[BUILT] [TESTED]

7. A bounded quantum loop
The design does not require a host-controlled while statement. The circuit contains a fixed maximum number of cycle blocks, each controlled by that path's current active bit:
active=1 → execute NAND A, B, and C
active=0 → leave scratch and later state unchangedTherefore circuit length is fixed, while path execution length is data. Different paths execute different numbers of cycles within the same circuit, and the history tape records where each path stopped. The circuit is statically unrolled, but its internal execution is path-dependent. That is the current hardware-compatible meaning of a coherent loop.

8. Exact verified results
The program contains an independent classical truth model and two quantum verification modes: exact marginal probabilities via Aer’s matrix-product-state simulator, and shot sampling of the fully measured circuit.
Verified on:
Python 3.11 and 3.12
Qiskit 2.5.0
Qiskit Aer 0.17.2
qiskit-ibm-runtime 0.48.0Four-cycle history result
python rauf_coherent_nand_loop.py --rounds 4 --counter history --shots 1024Exact:
M0: cycles=4, state=1, active=1, scratch=clean → 0.500000000000
M1: cycles=1, state=0, active=0, scratch=clean → 0.500000000000Sampled (seeded):
M0: cycles=4, state=1, active=1, scratch=clean → 504 / 1024
M1: cycles=1, state=0, active=0, scratch=clean → 520 / 1024Sixteen logical qubits; Aer compilation depth 356.
Sixteen-cycle history result
python rauf_coherent_nand_loop.py --rounds 16 --counter history --shots 256Exact:
M0: cycles=16, state=1, active=1, scratch=clean → 0.500000000000
M1: cycles=1, state=0, active=0, scratch=clean → 0.500000000000Sampled (seeded):
M0: cycles=16, state=1, active=1, scratch=clean → 128 / 256
M1: cycles=1, state=0, active=0, scratch=clean → 128 / 256Forty logical qubits;
